activities_projects.title: Analysis of Charged Particles
activities_projects.objective: To establish the existence of sub-atomic charged particles through static electricity.
steps: Perform Activity 4.1 by rubbing a comb through dry hair and a glass rod with silk.
Observe the attraction of neutral paper bits and the repulsion/attraction of the balloon.
Discuss how the transfer of electrons causes these physical observations.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that atoms are not indivisible and contain at least one sub-atomic particle (the electron).
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain the role of friction in revealing the internal charge of an atom (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Modeling Thomson’s Atomic Structure
activities_projects.objective: To visualize the distribution of positive and negative charges in the first atomic model.
steps: Construct a 3D model where a positive sphere of clay holds 'embedded' bead electrons.
Compare the model to a Christmas pudding or a watermelon.
Analyze why this model predicted an electrically neutral atom.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands J.J. Thomson’s postulate that an atom consists of a positively charged sphere with electrons embedded in it.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why Thomson's model failed to explain later scattering experiments (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Rutherford’s Alpha-Particle Simulation
activities_projects.objective: To understand the discovery of the atomic nucleus.
steps: Roll balls toward a target and record how many go straight, deflect slightly, or rebound.
Relate the 'rebound' (1 in 12,000) to the concentrated mass in the center of the atom.
Discuss why a gold foil was chosen for its extreme thinness.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that the nucleus is very small, dense, and positively charged compared to the rest of the atom.
activities_projects.evaluation: List three features of the nuclear model of an atom based on Rutherford's experiment (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Bohr’s Discrete Orbits Chart
activities_projects.objective: To map the energy levels and shells of an atom.
steps: Draw the nucleus and the K, L, M, and N shells.
Label the shells with their principal quantum numbers (n = 1, 2, 3, 4).
Discuss why electrons do not radiate energy while revolving in these discrete orbits.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands Neils Bohr's contribution to overcoming the limitations of Rutherford's model.
activities_projects.evaluation: Define 'discrete orbits' as described in Bohr’s postulates (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The $2n^2$ Electron Distribution Rule
activities_projects.objective: To apply the Bohr-Bury scheme for electron filling.
steps: Calculate the maximum capacity for the first four shells using $2n^2$.
Determine the maximum number of electrons allowed in the outermost orbit.
Explain why shells are filled in a 'step-wise' manner.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of the rules governing the arrangement of electrons in an atom.
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the electron capacity of the M shell (n=3) (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Diagramming the First 18 Elements
activities_projects.objective: To visualize the atomic structure of common elements.
steps: Draw the atomic structure for Helium, Carbon, Neon, and Sodium.
Distribute electrons correctly into K, L, and M shells.
Check the stability of atoms with 'filled' outermost shells like Helium and Neon.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Ability to represent the electronic configuration of elements graphically.
activities_projects.evaluation: Draw the schematic atomic structure of a Chlorine atom (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Calculating Valency from Octets
activities_projects.objective: To determine an atom's combining capacity based on its valence shell.
steps: Count valence electrons for elements like Oxygen, Fluorine, and Magnesium.
Apply the '8 minus valence electrons' rule for elements with more than 4 electrons.
Discuss why inert gases have zero valency.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that valency is determined by the number of electrons needed to complete an octet.
activities_projects.evaluation: Find the valency of Oxygen and explain the calculation (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomic Mass vs. Atomic Number Table
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate between the identity and the weight of an atom.
steps: Define Z (Atomic Number) as the number of protons.
Define A (Mass Number) as the total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons).
Practice the notation ${}_{Z}^{A}X$ for elements like Nitrogen and Oxygen.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Correctly identifies that protons define the element, while protons + neutrons define the mass.
activities_projects.evaluation: If an atom has 6 protons and 6 neutrons, find its Z and A (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotope Analysis: Average Atomic Mass
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the fractional atomic mass of elements with multiple isotopes.
materials: Data for Chlorine isotopes (35u at 75% and 37u at 25%)
steps: Set up the weighted average equation: $[(35 	imes 75/100) + (37 	imes 25/100)]$.
Calculate the result (35.5u).
Discuss why the mass of an element is taken as an average.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns why some atomic masses in the periodic table are not whole numbers.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the atomic mass of Chlorine is 35.5 u (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Applications of Isotopes and Isobars
activities_projects.objective: To explore the practical uses and differences of nuclear variants.
steps: Research the use of Uranium-235 in reactors and Cobalt-60 in medicine.
Contrast isotopes (same Z, different A) with isobars (different Z, same A).
Compare the chemical properties of isotopes versus their physical properties.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between isotopes and isobars and recognizes their industrial/medical value.
activities_projects.evaluation: Give one use each for an isotope of Iodine and an isotope of Uranium (10 marks).
